longest_path_nodes.name: Subtraction as taking away or separating
longest_path_nodes.subject: Mathematics

longest_path_nodes.name: Decomposing a shape into more equal shares
longest_path_nodes.subject: Mathematics

longest_path_nodes.name: Equivalent fractions on a number line
longest_path_nodes.subject: Mathematics

cross_subject_examples.reason: Interpreting cross-section diagrams of Earth builds on the skill of identifying 3-D shapes from 2-D representations
Recommending books with reasons and comparing across authors requires reflecting on one's reading experience — the universal learning-reflection habit applied to reading
Understanding that scientific knowledge changes over time requires the universal learning-reflection habit applied at the scale of a whole discipline
Cross-subject: interpreting categorical data by asking/answering questions relies on the English skill of asking relevant questions to seek information
Critiquing others' mathematical reasoning and explaining errors applies the universal error-analysis habit to peer arguments

cross_subject_examples.to_name: Different Accounts of the Same Event
cross_subject_examples.reason: Recognising that different people give different accounts of the same event is the historical application of the English skill of comparing and contrasting two texts on the same topic

cross_subject_examples.to_name: Different Accounts of the Same Event
cross_subject_examples.reason: Recognising that accounts diverge in systematic ways — based on who is telling the story — is an application of the universal pattern-recognition habit
Drawing ray diagrams requires measuring angles accurately with a protractor — the core skill in mt_4MFUAsbx_6
Giving feedback benefits from evaluating viewpoints and building on contributions
Identifying patterns in data is a form of the knowledge-transfer skill developed in Learning-to-Learn
Drawing conclusions that address a hypothesis with reference to evidence mirrors the English skill of drawing evidence from informational texts to support analysis
Cross-subject: communicating with mathematical precision at ages 10-11 benefits from understanding formal vs informal vocabulary register
Linking reading to own experiences is the English-domain application of the universal prior-knowledge activation habit
Using test results to make predictions and set up further tests is the science form of the universal generalisation habit — describing a pattern as a rule and testing it further
Naming what you are feeling is emotional comprehension monitoring — the universal habit of noticing what's happening inside applied to emotional experience

top_foundations.name: Building sentences
top_foundations.subject: English
top_foundations.description: Understand that words combine to make sentences — a sentence expresses a complete thought; produce and expand complete sentences in speech and writing

top_foundations.name: Animals Everywhere
top_foundations.subject: Science
top_foundations.description: Know that animals live all over the world — on land, in water, and in the air — and that every continent, even icy Antarctica, is home to animals, each suited to the conditions where they live

top_foundations.name: Addition as combining or putting together two
top_foundations.subject: Mathematics
top_foundations.description: Understand addition as combining or putting together two groups to find the total

top_foundations.name: The three digits of a three-digit number
top_foundations.subject: Mathematics
top_foundations.description: Understand that the three digits of a three-digit number represent amounts of hundreds, tens, and ones

top_foundations.name: Counting in 2s
top_foundations.subject: Mathematics
top_foundations.description: Count in multiples of 2, 5, and 10 (skip counting)

top_foundations.name: Writing Process Vocabulary
top_foundations.subject: English
top_foundations.description: Know and use the vocabulary of the writing process — compose, plan, draft, revise, edit, proofread, genre, audience, purpose, narrative, recount, instruction, paragraph, sequence, and detail — and understand that these words describe distinct steps and decisions that all writers make, not just tasks to tick off

top_foundations.name: The two digits of a two-digit number
top_foundations.subject: Mathematics
top_foundations.description: Understand that the two digits of a two-digit number represent amounts of tens and ones

top_foundations.name: Basic Nouns & Verbs
top_foundations.subject: English
top_foundations.description: Use frequently occurring nouns (people, places, things) and verbs (action words) appropriately in speech and writing

top_foundations.name: Writing Craft Vocabulary
top_foundations.subject: English
top_foundations.description: Know and use the vocabulary of writing craft and effect — form, structure, register, tone, voice, coherence, cohesion, argument, evidence, perspective, rhetoric, technique, formal, informal, and style — and understand that these words describe choices writers make intentionally to achieve a particular effect on the reader

top_foundations.name: Subordinate clauses
top_foundations.subject: English
top_foundations.description: Use subordination (when, if, that, because) and co-ordination (or, and, but) to join clauses and create compound and complex sentences

top_capstones.name: Earth's atmosphere
top_capstones.subject: Science
top_capstones.description: Develop a model to describe ways the geosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere interact as connected Earth systems

top_capstones.name: Habitats & Basic Needs
top_capstones.subject: Science
top_capstones.description: Identify that most living things live in habitats to which they are suited and describe how habitats provide for basic needs and how organisms depend on each other

top_capstones.name: Modern Archaeology and Egyptian Ethics
top_capstones.subject: History
top_capstones.description: Understand that modern Egyptologists use advanced technologies — CT scanning of mummies, satellite imagery to find buried structures, DNA analysis — alongside traditional excavation, and think critically about the ethics of archaeology: whether mummies should be displayed in museums, who owns ancient artefacts, and how colonial-era collecting affects how we study and present ancient Egypt today

top_capstones.name: Planning Ideas Before Writing
top_capstones.subject: English
top_capstones.description: Plan before writing by saying aloud or noting down what will be written, writing down ideas and key words, and encapsulating ideas sentence by sentence before composing

top_capstones.name: Persuasive Writing
top_capstones.subject: English
top_capstones.description: Write arguments to support claims with clear reasons and relevant evidence — including introducing claims, acknowledging counterclaims, organising reasons logically, maintaining a formal style, and providing a concluding statement

top_capstones.name: Evidence Versus Interpretation
top_capstones.subject: History
top_capstones.description: Distinguish between historical evidence and historical interpretation — evidence is what survived, interpretation is the argument a historian builds from it, and the same evidence can support different arguments

top_capstones.name: Polar Animals
top_capstones.subject: Science
top_capstones.description: Explore animals of the Arctic and Antarctic — polar bears, Arctic foxes, and walruses in the north; penguins, seals, and albatrosses in the south — and understand how polar animals survive extreme cold through thick fur or blubber, huddling behaviour, and seasonal changes like white winter coats

top_capstones.name: Structural Adaptations
top_capstones.subject: Science
top_capstones.description: Understand that animals have structural adaptations (body features like the giraffe's long neck, eagle's talons, dolphin's streamlined shape), behavioural adaptations (migration, hibernation, tool use), and physiological adaptations (antifreeze in Arctic fish blood, echolocation in bats) — and that these developed over many generations through natural selection

top_capstones.name: Changing Scientific Knowledge
top_capstones.subject: Science
top_capstones.description: Evaluate competing scientific explanations about dinosaurs by weighing fossil evidence — understanding that scientific knowledge changes as new fossils are discovered and new methods of analysis are developed

top_capstones.name: Grouping Living Things
top_capstones.subject: Science
top_capstones.description: Recognise that living things can be grouped in a variety of ways based on observable features

top_centrality.name: One-to-one counting
top_centrality.subject: Mathematics
top_centrality.description: One-to-one correspondence when counting objects: each object is paired with exactly one number name

top_centrality.name: How Many in Total?
top_centrality.subject: Mathematics
top_centrality.description: Cardinality principle: the last number said when counting a set tells how many objects are in the set, regardless of arrangement or order counted

top_centrality.name: Sitting and holding a pencil
top_centrality.subject: English
top_centrality.description: Sit correctly at a table holding a pencil comfortably and correctly; form lower-case letters in the correct direction starting and finishing in the right place

top_centrality.name: Subtraction as taking away or separating
top_centrality.subject: Mathematics
top_centrality.description: Understand subtraction as taking away or separating from a group to find how many remain

top_centrality.name: Reading and writing numbers to 20
top_centrality.subject: Mathematics
top_centrality.description: Read and write numerals from 0 to 20

top_centrality.name: Addition as combining or putting together two
top_centrality.subject: Mathematics
top_centrality.description: Understand addition as combining or putting together two groups to find the total

top_centrality.name: Modern Archaeology and Egyptian Ethics
top_centrality.subject: History
top_centrality.description: Understand that modern Egyptologists use advanced technologies — CT scanning of mummies, satellite imagery to find buried structures, DNA analysis — alongside traditional excavation, and think critically about the ethics of archaeology: whether mummies should be displayed in museums, who owns ancient artefacts, and how colonial-era collecting affects how we study and present ancient Egypt today

top_centrality.name: Rote counting to 100
top_centrality.subject: Mathematics
top_centrality.description: Rote count forwards and backwards from 0 to 100, beginning from 0, 1, or any given number, by ones

top_centrality.name: Division as equal sharing
top_centrality.subject: Mathematics
top_centrality.description: Understand division as sharing equally into groups or as grouping (how many groups of a given size can be made)
